pages.title: What is Neuroartan
pages.description: Neuroartan builds governed cognitive infrastructure for continuity, memory, profile identity, and structured intelligence.

sections.title: Definition
sections.body: Neuroartan is a product and infrastructure company building ICOS as a governed cognitive operating system rather than a generic chatbot.

sections.title: Public position
sections.body: The system is designed around continuity, structured memory, personal model identity, privacy boundaries, and safety governance.

sections.title: Communication boundary
sections.body: Public language should be clear, commercially coherent, and grounded in product capability without making claims of consciousness, therapy, medical authority, or guaranteed outcomes.

pages.title: What is ICOS
pages.description: ICOS is Neuroartan’s Intelligence Cognitive Operating System for profile continuity, memory, voice, reflection, and structured intelligence.

sections.title: Product definition
sections.body: ICOS is a cognitive operating system designed to help a user structure thought, memory, voice, continuity, reflection, and profile identity over time.

sections.title: What ICOS does
sections.body: ICOS supports a profile, a canonical personal model, private thought capture, public expression through Posts, memory development, voice and style training, and governed public identity controls.

sections.title: What ICOS is not
sections.body: ICOS is not positioned as a generic chatbot, a therapy product, a consciousness claim, a professional certification system, or an unlimited bot-creation platform.

pages.title: Platform
pages.description: Understand the Neuroartan platform as the public product layer for ICOS, profile identity, model continuity, and user-facing controls.

sections.title: Platform role
sections.body: The Neuroartan platform is the public interface layer for creating a profile, developing a canonical personal model, managing memory, publishing Posts, and controlling visibility.

sections.title: Design direction
sections.body: The platform should feel unified, restrained, high-trust, and operationally clear across product, documentation, verification, privacy, and account surfaces.

sections.body: Advanced capabilities such as economy, marketplace, payouts, hiring, and inter-model coordination remain blocked until governance, legal, privacy, security, and product review.

pages.title: Product Capabilities
pages.description: Explore the core capabilities of ICOS: profile, memory, voice, model identity, Posts, verification, safety, and continuity.

sections.title: Profile and identity
sections.body: A profile is the user’s canonical public or private identity surface. It connects the user, their Posts, their model identity, and their continuity controls.

sections.title: Memory and reflection
sections.body: ICOS is designed to support long-term memory, thought structure, reflection, pattern recognition, and continuity over time.

sections.title: Voice and style
sections.body: Voice training allows the system to learn a user’s style, language, tone, and expression within approved privacy and consent boundaries.

sections.title: Trust systems
sections.body: Verification, safety, anti-impersonation, device integrity, appeal, and restriction systems protect public trust and model identity.

pages.title: Plans & Subscriptions
pages.description: Compare Free, Pro, and Pro Max boundaries, including canonical model depth and verification eligibility.

sections.title: Plan principle
sections.body: Every profile receives one canonical personal model. Paid tiers deepen that model rather than creating additional canonical personal models.

sections.title: Verification eligibility
sections.body: Verification submission begins at Pro and remains review-based. Pro or Pro Max membership does not guarantee verification approval.

sections.title: Economy boundary
sections.body: Marketplace, payouts, hiring, public ranking, inter-model coordination, and posthumous economy remain blocked until review.

pages.title: Error Reference
pages.description: The error reference defines traceable public error codes for product, account, verification, device integrity, connector, API, billing, and documentation issues.

sections.title: Error code standard
sections.body: Public error codes should be stable, searchable, and safe to disclose. Each code should include a category prefix, short title, public cause, fix path, and escalation condition.

sections.title: Initial code families
sections.body: Recommended public code families include AUTH for authentication, ACC for account, VER for verification, DEV for device integrity, API for integration, CLI for command-line workflows, BILL for billing, DOC for documentation, and SAFE for trust and safety review states.

sections.title: Private boundary
sections.body: Error documentation must not reveal fraud signals, device-risk scoring, enforcement thresholds, internal evidence, investigation tooling, private account state, or evasion-sensitive mechanics.

sections.title: Support use
sections.body: Support and developer teams should reference public error codes when writing help articles, release notes, CLI output, API responses, and user-facing diagnostics.
